name: Inswinging Near-Post Flick-On
description:
Inswinging delivery to the near post. Target man (usually a tall striker or midfielder) makes a run from the edge of the six-yard box to attack the near post and flick the ball on towards the back post or far corner. Secondary runner attacks the back post late.

Trigger: taker points to near post before delivery.
Key personnel: near-post flicker (6ft+), back-post runner, penalty-spot attacker for second ball.

name: Outswinging Far-Post Delivery
description:
Outswinging corner delivered to the far post. Two runners cross: one dummy-runs near post pulling a defender, the second attacks the far post for a header or cut-back. Third man positions at the penalty spot for a second-ball volley.

Key risk: delivery needs to clear the first defender at the near post. Effective against zonal defences that shift late.

name: Short Corner — Overload & Cut-Back
description:
Taker plays short to a support runner wide. Defence must commit a marker, creating an overload. Support player drives to the byline and cuts back low across the six-yard box for a tap-in. Penalty-area runners time their arrival to the cut-back.

Works best when opposition uses man-marking: pulling one marker to the short runner opens lanes for arriving runners.

name: Driven Low Corner — Penalty Spot
description:
Hard, flat delivery aimed at the penalty spot rather than the near or far post. Attackers time runs to arrive at pace. Effective against defences that set up deep and block high-ball routes. Requires a technically precise delivery.

Risks: if delivery is off-target, counter-attack exposure is high. Blind-side runners from deep can exploit disorganised zonal shape.

name: Corner — Blocking Routine (2-Man Screen)
description:
Two attackers position themselves just outside the six-yard box. On the taker's run-up they step across and screen two zonal defenders, creating a free lane for the primary target runner arriving from deep.

Legal provided contact is minimal. Most effective against teams that set both posts zonal and do not switch-track screens. Must be rehearsed — timing is everything.

name: Zonal Defence — 6-Man Wall
description:
Six defenders occupy zones across the six-yard box. Two post-players mark near and far post. One player attacks every ball in the zone without waiting for an attacker to arrive first.

Strengths: removes mismatch risk, predictable assignments.
Weaknesses: vulnerable to blockers/screens and second-ball from edge of box. Assign one midfielder to edge of area for second ball.

name: Man-Marking Defence — Ticket System
description:
Each defender is assigned a specific attacker ('ticket') regardless of movement. Goalkeeper owns the near post. Two free defenders cover back post and penalty spot for any untracked runners.

Strengths: eliminates second-ball free men, individual accountability.
Weaknesses: susceptible to crossing/screening runs that pull markers away from dangerous zones. Pre-match: identify the opposition's primary aerial threat and assign the best aerial defender to them.

name: Direct FK — Wall + Curl
description:
Single taker. Wall set 9.15m away. Delivery curled over or around the wall into the far corner. GK must cover near post: delivery targets top-far corner exploiting GK's position.

Pre-requisite: dead-ball specialist with consistent curl from 20-25m. Most effective when taker's natural curve takes ball away from the GK's dive direction.

name: Direct FK — Dipping Drive Under the Wall
description:
Player in the wall ducks/jumps on the taker's signal to create a gap. Taker drives low under the jumping player's legs. Requires rehearsal with a trusted wall-player.

Alternative: two takers approach; one runs over the ball, the second drives through the gap created by distraction. Works within 22m of goal.

name: Indirect FK — Dummy Run + Shooter Arrives Late
description:
Two players stand over the ball. First runs over without touching — wall jumps in anticipation. Second player rolls ball sideways 2m to a third runner arriving late into shooting position before the wall resets.

Most effective from wide-central positions 20-28m out. The wall's premature jump creates a clear shooting lane.

name: Indirect FK — Near-Post Flick-On
description:
Inswinging cross-field delivery from wide FK position towards the near post. Target man flicks on; back-post runners and penalty-spot attackers time late arrivals.

Same principle as corner near-post routine but delivery arc is flatter. Particularly effective from wide right if taker has natural inswing with right foot.

name: FK Defence — 4-Man Wall + 2 Runners
description:
GK organises 4-man wall to cover near post. GK covers far side. Two players are released from the wall to press any short-ball options the moment the ball is played.

For wide FKs: reduce to 2-man wall, position 4 defenders in box to match opposition runners. Prioritise picking up the shooter arriving late rather than defending the initial delivery.

name: Long Throw-In — Target & Flick
description:
Long throw-in specialist launches ball into the penalty area to a tall target. Target wins first contact and flicks on to a secondary runner making a diagonal run behind the defence.

Threat zone: final third, wide right or left near the corner flag. Requires a throw-in specialist with 25m+ range and a reliable aerial target.

name: Throw-In — Quick Play & Combination
description:
Thrower plays quickly to feet of nearest player before the opposition press organises. Receiver lays off one-touch to a third player making a forward run into space.

Objective: gain territory and break lines rather than directly threaten goal. Most effective in middle third. Pre-requisite: players must scan and identify the passing lane before the throw is taken.

name: Throw-In — Defending Deep Block
description:
When opponent has a throw-in in the final third, defensive block drops into a compact low shape. Nearest player presses the receiver immediately on receipt. Second line holds shape to prevent the turn.

Do not press the thrower — press the first receiver. Watch for the overlap run behind the press: cover the channel with a second defender.
